As I've mentioned several times before, intentional strategic tactical listening is the most important skill in any negotiation. In hostage negotiations, we usually had four or five people whose only job was to listen for specific aspects of the communication. There's so much information there. They would pick up on information that the primary negotiator would completely missed. Most of the time people approach a situation thinking so hard about their arguments, they're unable to listen effectively to what the other person has to say. According to George Miller, we're only able to process about seven bits of information in our conscious mind at any given moment. That means that our minds are easily overwhelmed and miss out on tons of information. It just isn't possible to focus on what they're saying, how they're saying it, what they're talking about, their tone of voice, verbal clues, and so on. So don't be in a hurry. Keep them talking. Now here's some things about the important voice which I'll specifically lay out for you. It has to do with tone and inflection and the fact that how we say something is typically five times more important than what we say. Now there are three types of voices. The assertive voice, which is direct and honest, the positive playful voice, and the FM DJ voice. Now you combine that with our two types of inflections, which are inquisitive, hear the upward inflection like I'm genuinely asking an honest question, or declarative, as if I'm simply making a statement. Those two inflections combined with the three voices give you incredible power. Please click on one of the three types of voices and two inflections in order to learn more about them.
